context: The operator asked whether two existing specs (the single-lane slice, the live-activity scenario) had implemented the foundation well enough or whether a third spec was warranted. The model had just read both specs' the requirements doc files, which state intended scope. The repo also contained an the implementation report for the single-lane slice and the regression notes (a task regression seal) for the live-activity scenario recording what was actually built and proven.
options: Assess coverage from the two the requirements doc docs alone (what was intended/specified)
Also read the implementation-report and regression-notes to learn what was actually proven versus merely claimed before judging
choice: Also read the single-lane slice/the implementation report and the live-activity scenario/the regression notes to ground the verdict in proven outcomes.
rationale: Requirements state intent; only the outcome records show what shipped, what was deferred, and what carried forward as residual risk. A coverage verdict built on intent alone would assert capabilities that may have been disconfirmed in implementation (verify-before-asserting). The cost of two extra reads is trivial against the cost of a wrong did-we-do-enough verdict that the operator would act on. This let the later assessment cite concrete facts like the PATCH 422 no-op and the LOUD-SKIPPED cadence legs rather than restating the spec.
outcome: The t25 verdict drew directly on those records (one write edge / order toggle, PATCH disconfirmed upstream as 422 no-op, monitor mode carried-not-dropped, batch/external-message signals unmapped), producing a far more specific assessment than requirements alone would allow.

context: After reading both specs and their outcome records, the model had to answer the operator's actual question: Is the foundation implemented enough, or is a third spec justified — and if so, for what. The foundation locks a switchable control/monitor operating model (decision #4); both shipped slices ran control-only and listed monitor mode as carried-not-dropped, and only one write edge (the order toggle) had been proven against the 'supervisor edits logic' thesis. This is a purpose/direction call the operator owns.
options: Conclude the two slices did enough and recommend moving to implementation
Conclude a third spec is justified and unilaterally pick its scope
Conclude a third spec is justified, but rank the open foundation surface and lean toward monitor mode while leaving the final pick to the operator
choice: Recommend a third spec is justified and present the open surface as a ranked list, leading with monitor mode + read allowlist as the strongest candidate.
rationale: Whether to spend on a third spec and what it should cover is a direction/taste call that belongs to the operator, so the model frames options with a marked lean rather than deciding alone. The lean is principled, not arbitrary: Monitor mode is the only LOCKED decision (#4) with an unbuilt half, and §9 already resolved its design path (client-side read allowlist modeled on the read-allowlist module), so it is the lowest-risk highest-value gap. Grounding the verdict in the foundation's own criterion (two modes; control is thin at one write edge, monitor is absent) keeps it auditable. Presenting a ranked surface lets the operator redirect scope without the model having pre-committed it.
outcome: Operator replied 'good' (t26) and at t47 directed the model to draft a spec prompt using the ranked candidates, confirming both the verdict and the lean were accepted.

context: The model was wiring the newly vendored the AI-editor MCP editor plugin into the client by copying vendor/the AI-editor/plugin/addons into client/addons. The addon is third-party tooling vendored into the tree. The model paused explicitly on whether the new client/addons directory should be added to .gitignore before committing the copy.
options: Add client/addons (or the AI-editor plugin) to .gitignore so the vendored tool is not tracked
Leave it tracked, and verify its current gitignore status rather than assume
choice: Leave the addon tracked, verifying with git check-ignore that it is not already ignored.
rationale: The plugin is a dev-workflow dependency the next session must have present and enabled via the engine project file, so excluding it would silently break the editor bridge for anyone who checks out the repo. Rather than assume the ignore state, the model ran git check-ignore -v in the same command to confirm it factually (verify-before-asserting). Tracking is the reversible default here — a stray ignore rule is the harder-to-debug failure. The check made the subsequent 'not gitignored' claim verifiable instead of presumed.
outcome: Check-ignore returned exit=1 (not ignored); the model reported 'Addon copied and not gitignored' at t67 and proceeded to enable the plugin in the engine project file and register the MCP endpoint.

context: The model was establishing the read-only / monitor-mode story for the foundation doc. The supervisor runs unauthenticated on loopback with no read-only token; the dashboard enforces read-only client-side via a default-deny proxy allowlist that strips an X-CLI-request header. The model was about to characterize what the 3D view monitor mode could safely assume.
options: Assume GET requests are inherently safe and treat any GET as allowed in monitor mode
Verify against the allowlist/specs and account for side-effecting GETs (e.g. An agent-priming GET) before asserting monitor-mode safety
choice: Verify against the specs and treat some GETs as side-effecting rather than assuming GET is safe.
rationale: Verify-before-asserting: The naive HTTP intuition that GET is read-only is a claim that would propagate into the foundation doc and later the spec, so it had to be checked against the actual API rather than assumed. The model grepped the security spec and read the allowlist source, finding the supervisor has no read-only scope and that the safety guarantee lives entirely in a client-side allowlist. It explicitly concluded monitor mode needs an explicit read-endpoint allowlist modeled on the dashboard's, not a blanket GET pass. Getting this wrong would have written an unsafe assumption (reads are free) into a spec-grade document.
outcome: Foundation §9 was updated (t67) to state no supervisor auth/scopes exist and monitor mode must be client-enforced via an explicit allowlist accounting for side-effecting GETs.

context: The model had finished mapping the dashboard's wire contract, write surface, SSE specifics, and read-only model. The operator's t1 request was to 'analyze it to understand if we could gather insights for our foundation document.' The findings were directly relevant to several foundation sections and even superseded one existing pointer.
options: Proceed to edit ai_docs/the foundation doc directly with the resolved findings
Report findings organized by which sections they affect and propose concrete amendments without editing the document
choice: Report findings organized by affected section and propose amendments without editing the document yet.
rationale: Don't-widen-scope: The operator framed this as an analysis question, not an instruction to modify the canonical doc, so editing would overreach the asked-for boundary. The model explicitly noted it should 'propose concrete amendments without editing the document itself' and surface a verdict. Treating the analysis and the writeback as separate, operator-gated steps keeps a high-value canonical document from being mutated on an implied mandate. It also let the operator confirm the verdict before any edit landed.
outcome: The model delivered a structured report with a verdict; the operator then explicitly authorized edits at t41 ('proceed refactoring our the foundation doc'), confirming the gating was correct.

context: Mid-refactor of the foundation doc, the model had just edited the §2 table and added the control-plane client-dashboard row. It noticed Prettier had reformatted the table's column widths and would likely also rewrap the prose sections it was about to edit, which were originally hand-wrapped.
options: Continue applying the queued Edits against the content already in context (now stale after Prettier rewrapped it)
Re-read the affected regions of the foundation doc to get the post-Prettier state before issuing the next exact-match edits
choice: Re-read the affected regions before making the next edits.
rationale: Exact-string edits fail or corrupt when old_string no longer matches the on-disk text, and Prettier had demonstrably mutated formatting the model did not author. Verifying the current file state is far cheaper than debugging a failed or mis-targeted edit and far safer than silently editing the wrong span. The model treated the formatter as an external mutator and reconciled to ground truth before proceeding rather than trusting its cached view. This is verify-(file-state)-before-acting under a known environment surprise.
outcome: The model re-read the file (t53) and the subsequent §6/§7/§8/§9 edits all applied successfully with no exact-match failures.

context: The requirements architect returned four open questions (OQ-1..4) about writeback target, head-seq capture technique, item actor/subject attribution, and the edit-changed-next-action signal. The workflow allows surfacing these to the operator for resolution before design. The model judged most were resolvable from material already on disk (the foundation doc, the OpenAPI contract, and the feature request itself).
options: Surface all four open questions to the operator and wait for answers
Resolve what is answerable from the foundation doc and the OpenAPI contract, flag only genuine residue
choice: Resolve the open questions from on-disk material rather than asking the operator.
rationale: OQ-1 was already settled by the feature request's explicit choice of patches-primary/order-fallback, and OQ-3 is settled by construction (single agent + single item makes attribution unambiguous). OQ-2 and OQ-4 are answerable by reading the OpenAPI spec and the definition of done. Asking the operator about facts the model can verify itself wastes a round-trip and pushes work the operator delegated back onto them. Verify-before-asserting: Read the contract, then only escalate true unknowns.
outcome: All four resolved from disk; the model presented resolutions in t22 and the operator approved proceeding to design at t23.

context: The design synthesizer flagged a load-bearing open question R-1: Foundation §7a (verified against the working dashboard) says the patches subsystem exposes PromptTemplate, but the synthesizer suspected the OpenAPI PUT body omits it. The model received this as a self-reported finding and must decide whether to accept it or check it before presenting the design for approval.
options: Accept the synthesizer's R-1 claim and present it as-is
Independently query the OpenAPI spec to confirm whether prompt_template is in the PUT body before presenting
choice: Independently verify R-1 against the OpenAPI contract before presenting.
rationale: R-1 is load-bearing — it determines whether the primary writeback path is even viable — so an unchecked claim could mislead the approval decision. The check is a cheap jq query against a checked-in spec; the error of presenting a wrong primary path is expensive downstream. Cost-asymmetry favors the check. Verify-before-asserting on any claim the model is about to relay to the operator as ground truth.
outcome: Confirmed: AgentPatchSetInputBody exposes only 7 fields (dir/env/name/scope/suspended/tmux_alias/work_dir), no prompt_template, versus AgentPatch's 48 fields — a real spec/payload mismatch. Reported at t32.

context: To start the implement loop, the model needs a running Gas instance supervisor. The supervisor isn't running and no cli binary exists; the source is vendored at vendor/the control-plane client with Go 1.24.1 available. A locked invariant requires consuming Gas instance as an unmodified black box. Building from vendored source via `make build` (CGO/the datastore deps, potentially slow) is the path to a live supervisor.
options: Build cli from the vendored source via make build
Treat the absence of a running supervisor as a blocker and halt for the operator
choice: Build cli from the vendored source to bring up a real supervisor.
rationale: Building from unmodified vendored source does not violate the black-box invariant — it consumes Gas instance as shipped without patching it. A real loopback supervisor is a hard prerequisite for the no-mocks tasks, so resolving it now keeps the loop moving rather than surfacing an avoidable blocker. The build is reversible environment setup, not a source change. Resolving the blocker is preferable to escalating something the model can do itself.
outcome: Build dispatched in background (id b3bvleffp at t59); completed by t69 and the supervisor came up serving localhost:8372 (a stray process), enabling the task's real REST read.

context: The operator authorized parallel task execution when file footprints don't overlap, overriding the single-agent-at-a-time default. A task is stuck on an environmental blocker (no agent sessions spawn) while many independent tasks (004/006, then 005/007) touch disjoint files. The model must decide whether to fan out concurrent pipelines and how to prevent collisions.
options: Keep one agent at a time (the prior default)
Fan out parallel pipelines on disjoint file footprints with an explicit non-overlap contract per agent
choice: Fan out parallel pipelines, each with a disjoint-footprint and own-checkbox-line contract.
rationale: The operator explicitly lifted the serialization constraint conditioned on non-conflicting work, so parallelism is now sanctioned where footprints are disjoint. The chief hazard is concurrent edits to shared files (tasks.md checkboxes, tests/run_tests.sh); the model mitigates structurally by instructing each agent to flip only its own checkbox line and anchor appends defensively. Treating the supervisor as shared infra with retry-on-refused contains the other cross-cutting resource. This trades a small coordination cost for large throughput on an 17-task graph.
outcome: Multiple pipelines ran concurrently (e.g. T130-132, t137, t142, t156, t176) with no reported file-clash; tasks 004/005/006/007/008/009/010/011/012 all landed without checkbox or run_tests.sh corruption.

context: Agent sessions die instantly on spawn ("session died during startup"), blocking the task's worker lifecycle. The model traced it: The tmux server's global PATH lacks the cli/svc/the datastore dirs, so the agent start_command resolves `cli` to /usr/bin/cli (an unrelated system package) and crashes. Options are a per-agent workaround or a server-level fix; killing/restarting the tmux server would disrupt other live user sessions.
options: Make each agent's start_command robust with absolute paths / per-agent PATH env
Prepend the cli/svc/the datastore dirs to the tmux server's global PATH (one structural fix)
Kill and restart the tmux server with a corrected PATH
choice: Prepend the tool dirs to the tmux server's global PATH via set-environment.
rationale: The root cause is one shared misconfiguration (server PATH), so a single set-environment fixes every current and future agent pane at once — structural-fix over patching each agent's start_command instance by instance. Killing the tmux server was rejected because it would disrupt unrelated live user sessions (recon-pool, etc.). Editing every agent.toml would be an instance-fix that leaves the next agent equally broken. The server-PATH change is the smallest edit that addresses the class of failure.
outcome: After the PATH fix plus a supervisor restart (t116), worker sessions spawned with outcome=success duration~4.4s; the deadlock was cleared, though the worker still didn't claim the item (a separate fixture issue handed to the task finisher).

context: The task ClientErrorChannel bundle is in. The model must choose the GDScript shape for the error sink. An autoload singleton (instance state + signals) aligns with how later consumers might subscribe, but requires editing the shared the engine project file that a task may also touch. A static class_name with shared state avoids that edit.
options: Autoload singleton with instance state and signals
Static class_name ClientErrorChannel with static report()/get_reports()/clear()
choice: Static class_name with static state (no autoload, no the engine project file edit).
rationale: The interface the design actually requires is just report(component, op, message) — signals are speculative since nothing currently subscribes. The static form avoids editing the shared the engine project file, removing a concurrent-edit hazard while parallel pipelines run, and it matches the existing CliConfig global pattern for consistency. Don't-widen-scope: Pick the simplest shape that satisfies the contract; signal support can be added later if a real subscriber appears. Godot 4.7-beta supports static vars fine.
outcome: Executor implemented class_name ClientErrorChannel with static state; 13/13 unit assertions passed, runner skip-gating worked, and the reviewer approved at t150 with only a minor coverage advisory.

context: The task SnapshotLoader bundle surfaced a contract gap: AgentResponse on the wire has no `tags` or `prompt` fields, but a requirement and the task's cold-start recovery prop mapping expect to derive props from name/tags/prompt/pool. The model must decide how scene-gen obtains prop-driving attributes.
options: Surface only what the wire actually provides (name/pool/rig/model/provider/display_name/description + item labels)
Invent a per-agent detail fetch to recover tags/prompt
choice: Surface wire truth as-is; a task derives props from the available config set, no invented fetches.
rationale: Inventing a per-agent detail fetch adds an endpoint dependency that the contract doesn't support and risks fabricating fields the supervisor doesn't expose. Surfacing the wire's real config set keeps the snapshot an honest black-box read and still gives cold-start recovery enough attributes to map props generically. Don't-widen-scope and honesty-to-the-wire over convenience. The cold-start recovery principle (props a pure function of whatever config exists) tolerates a reduced attribute set.
outcome: A task implemented surfacing wire fields only; a task mapped props via hash-over-attributes with no role table and was approved at t219 (cold-start recovery audit clean).

context: The task SSE-framer bundle revealed the OpenAPI declares a second named frame, `event: Heartbeat`, in addition to `event: Event`. The model must decide how the framer treats heartbeats and how to structure the framer so its grammar is testable. Feeding heartbeats into the EnvelopeParser would generate a spurious malformed-report on every heartbeat.
options: Route all frames (including heartbeat) through the EnvelopeParser
Filter heartbeat frames out and split a pure byte-chunk framer from the socket transport
choice: Exclude heartbeat from the parser and split a pure framer (sse_frame_reader) from the HTTPClient transport.
rationale: Heartbeats carry no envelope, so passing them to a tolerant parser that reports typeless frames would flood ClientErrorChannel with false faults — a correctness bug masquerading as resilience. Separating the byte-chunk framer from the socket transport makes the SSE grammar (multi-line frames, chunk-boundary reassembly) pure-unit testable without opening a socket. This is a structural call: A transport-agnostic seam pays off for every later framing edge. The split also lets long-poll reuse the same envelope edge.
outcome: Executor built the event-source module + pure the SSE frame-reader module + the SSE event-source module with one _on_frame path; 44 pure-unit assertions passed (t226). The pure split is what later let the UTF-8 fix be unit-tested.

context: The task review returned a blocking finding: Sse_frame_reader.feed() decodes each chunk independently, so a multibyte UTF-8 character split across a chunk boundary corrupts to U+FFFD in both halves (probe-verified). Live envelopes carry non-ASCII free text. The model must decide the scope and shape of the fix.
options: Re-implement the frame reader
Smallest in-place fix: Buffer bytes across chunk boundaries and decode once per completed line, with a regression test
choice: Smallest in-place byte-buffering fix with a mid-character regression test.
rationale: Never-throw-away: The framer's structure is sound; only the decode site is wrong, so a surgical byte-level buffer is the minimal correct change rather than a rewrite that would re-introduce risk. Adding a failing-first regression test pins the bug so it can't silently return. The fix exploits a UTF-8 invariant (continuation bytes 0x80-0xBF never equal CR/LF), so scanning bytes for terminators is provably safe. Structural-fix at the one decode site beats patching symptoms.
outcome: Fix landed with TDD evidence (new multibyte tests failed against old code on exactly the two round-trip assertions, passed after); scoped re-review at t260 approved it with mutation probe confirming, plus CR/LF-straddle/lone-CR/empty-chunk edges clean (t262).

context: The task bundle surfaced a tension: ADR-0003 specifies signal selection "by type-prefix," but claim and block both arrive as `item.updated` discriminated by payload.item.status, so a literal type-prefix function can't tell them apart. The design record is now inconsistent with the verified wire reality. The model must decide how to reconcile.
options: Regenerate the design/ADR-0003 to fix the inconsistency
Adopt a semantic-enum classify() (type-family prefix + payload.item.status sub-read) and append a short content-preserving amendment note to ADR-0003
choice: Adopt the classify() framing and append a surgical amendment note to ADR-0003.
rationale: A wholesale regeneration would churn an otherwise-correct ADR and risk dropping prior decisions; a small append-only amendment preserves the record while documenting the prefix+status-discrimination split — the same surgical precedent set by the ADR-0005/0007 amendment. Structural-fix kept minimal: Change only the part the wire reality contradicts. Keeping the registry total while routing claim/block through one item.updated family preserves the build-gate law. Don't-widen-scope on design edits.
outcome: Executor implemented the in-scope-event module + the signal-registry module with the classify() split and appended the ADR-0003 amendment note; the totality gate's own mutation self-check bit (commenting ITEM_BLOCKED failed 3 assertions), 47/47 passed (t272), review dispatched at t275.

context: With a task and a task executors actively writing the main scene script and client/pipeline/ files, a task (SignalQueue/Player) becomes the convergence task and its interfaces are mostly locked. The model could dispatch the task context-synthesizer now to keep the pipeline saturated, as it had been doing for other read-only bundles ahead of dependency completion.
options: Dispatch the task bundle now to stay ahead
Hold the task bundle until a task and a task land
choice: Hold the task bundle until 011 and 012 finish writing.
rationale: The task's bundle agent would read the main scene script and client/pipeline/ — the exact files the two running executors are mid-write on — so synthesizing now risks capturing half-written state and producing a bundle built on a stale or inconsistent surface. Unlike the earlier get-ahead dispatches (which read already-landed interfaces), here the inputs are actively churning. Cost-asymmetry: A brief wait is cheaper than a bundle built on corrupt inputs that misleads the executor. Reversible-first: Waiting loses nothing but a few minutes.
outcome: Model deliberately deferred the task bundle (t255/t256); the session hit its limit at t276-279 before a task was dispatched, so this part ends with 011/012 still resolving.

context: The task gap-free startup integration test passed all assertions but the model noticed applied sequences 17089-17091 were at/below the snapshot baseline of 17090, meaning overlapping envelopes were double-applied when the startup buffer was empty at drain time and the cursor stayed uninitialized. Subagents were dying instantly on a session limit, but the model's own tool calls still worked.
options: Fix the cursor-baseline-seed defect directly inline in the main scene script
Wait for the session limit to reset and dispatch a task-executor to make the fix
choice: Fix the cursor-baseline-seed defect directly inline in the main scene script
rationale: The change was one-to-few lines (seed the cursor with the snapshot baseline at drain so live envelopes already in the snapshot are dropped) plus a test re-run, so the cost of doing it inline was low. Dispatching an executor was blocked anyway since agents were hitting the session limit and would fail instantly. Keeping momentum on a verified, small, reversible edit outweighed the workflow purity of routing it through an executor. The model explicitly accepted that this code would later be reviewed as unreviewed-by-default and disclosed it to the eventual a task reviewer.
outcome: Re-run of test_gapfree_startup passed all 6 assertions (baseline seq 17171); the later a task review specifically scrutinized the direct fix and proved it correct in all four edge cases.

context: The task reviewer flagged that the classifier maps 'claimed' strictly on status=="in_progress", but the live the demo environment ring showed only open/closed statuses on item.updated — raising the worry that the 'lift to lane' signal could never fire live. This fact would be passed to the in-flight a task executor.
options: Accept the reviewer's worry and pass 'the demo environment never projects in_progress' to a task as fact
Check the recorded rings directly before asserting whether in_progress ever projects
choice: Check the recorded rings/on-disk event logs directly before asserting
rationale: The reviewer had checked one instance (the demo environment) that never ran a worker lifecycle, so its absence of in_progress was not proof the projection never happens. Asserting a false wire-contract claim to the task executor would have caused it to build the wrong behavior. The check was cheap (read-only curl and grep against the file-backed recorder, no clash with running agents) versus the expensive error of mis-specifying the classifier contract — a verify-before-asserting / cost-asymmetry call. The model grepped the demo environment-r3's events.jsonl where the worker lifecycle had actually run.
outcome: Found three real item.updated envelopes with status:"in_progress" in the demo environment-r3 history; confirmed the claim does project on the wire and passed this corrected fact to the task executor.

context: Probing the wire, the model found that GET /v0/instance/{c}/events with index=281 returned events at seq 13711+ rather than a replay slice from 282, contradicting the exclusive-resume semantics it had given the in-flight a task executor in the context bundle. The OpenAPI description only said 'blocks until a newer event arrives'.
options: Trust the documented/bundled exclusive-resume index semantics
Read the vendor handler source to determine what index actually does before correcting the contract
choice: Read the vendor handler source (huma_handlers_events.go, blocking.go) to verify the real semantics
rationale: The empirical probe disconfirmed the bundle's assumption, and a task was built on it, so the correct contract was load-bearing. The OpenAPI text was ambiguous, so the authoritative answer lived in the handler code, not docs. Verifying against source before propagating a correction is verify-before-asserting; getting it wrong would have shipped a silently-broken resume path. The model read the fast-path block and confirmed index only gates blocking while the page is always the N newest events ascending, so the client must filter seq > cursor itself.
outcome: Confirmed index is a block-trigger only and the page is a trailing newest-N window (default 100, max 1000); the model documented the corrected contract and the independent a task executor arrived at the same correction, proven by a live exclusive-resume test.

context: The task's round-2 review returned changes-requested with a blocking cold-first-seed race: The controller strips the item's assignee ~12s after assignment, before the worker spawns, stalling the item open forever roughly 1-in-3 cold seeds. Before looping back to a fix executor, the model searched vendor source for the exact unassign mechanism but the on_death/on_boot hooks only matched in_progress items and ReleaseIfCurrent had no live callers.
options: Keep probing vendor source until the exact reconciler that clears the assignee is pinned
Time-box the root-cause hunt and dispatch a mechanism-agnostic verify-and-retry fixture fix
choice: Time-box the hunt and dispatch a mechanism-agnostic verify-and-retry fix in seed-item.sh
rationale: The model had spent several probes without pinning the reconciler, and judged the exact mechanism mattered less than a robust fixture-level fix. A poll-after-nudge loop that re-asserts the assignment if it's stripped while the item is still open is correct regardless of which controller path clears it — a structural fix at the fixture boundary rather than chasing an instance in opaque vendor code. Continuing to probe had diminishing returns against the session budget. The model documented the race window in the README and prescribed bounded retries with honest exit codes.
outcome: The round-3 fixer reported the retry loop fired 3x live on a fresh instance, catching the exact assignee-oscillation signature; the fix was later approved with the reviewer confirming it was bounded and could not double-close.

context: The task round-3 fixer returned blocked: Its retry-loop fix worked, but a host regression now killed every supervisor-spawned worker pane because an unrelated system package's /usr/bin/cli shadows the control-plane client's cli when the pane's login shell re-sources ~/.bashrc and reorders PATH. The executor asked the model to choose between removing an unrelated system package (a destructive sudo host change) or unfreezing packs/ to pin the worker's start_command.
options: Remove an unrelated system package from the host as the executor offered
Unfreeze packs/ and pin start_command to a robust cli resolution (the executor's other option)
Decline both as framed and require a portable mechanism (env-indirection or a templated launcher shim) verified against vendor source first
choice: Reject the destructive option, authorize a targeted packs/ unfreeze, and require a portability-verified mechanism
rationale: Removing an unrelated system package would be a destructive, non-reversible host change that leaves the fixture broken on any operator machine with a conflicting cli — the direct opposite of a requirement's fresh-operator reproducibility, so it fails the actual goal. A hardcoded absolute cli path breaks portability across checkouts. The model verified vendor source first (no {{.CliBin}} template var, no CLI_BIN session export, but {{.ProjectRoot}} expansion and shell-evaluated start_command are supported), then constrained the fix to a portable launcher-shim/env-indirection that keeps the bare-PATH fallback for clean hosts. Unfreezing packs/ for a one-line fixture detail was judged within spec intent, not reopening a locked §4 decision.
outcome: The continuation fixer empirically proved tmux runs the command through a shell and chose a launcher shim resolving the real the control-plane client binary (banner-discriminated against an unrelated system package); proven green on two cold instances and the demo environment's worker repaired in place; a task later approved with the reviewer confirming a silent wrong-binary exec is structurally impossible.

context: The task executor returned blocked: Implementation complete and unit-green (28/28), and its first live run did show the autonomous printer and stamp signals under 2s, but the live integration re-run hard-failed because the demo environment worker had stalled (the cli-shadow regression). A task continuation agent was already in flight to repair the worker in place.
options: Dispatch the task review now against the stalled instance
Hold the task review until the worker is repaired so the reviewer sees the full lifecycle live
choice: Hold the task review until the worker is repaired
rationale: A reviewer cannot verify the end-to-end acceptance criteria (the printer to lift to stamp triple) against an instance whose worker pane is dead, so dispatching now would burn a review cycle judging a known-environmental failure rather than the code. The stall had a known root cause already being repaired, making the wait bounded. Holding avoids a wasted round and a misleading verdict; the model used the wait productively by dispatching read-only context bundles for a task and a task instead of idling.
outcome: After the worker was repaired the model re-ran the live signals test itself (green), then dispatched the review, which approved a task with zero findings beyond one advisory nit.

context: The task (writeback) context bundle landed. The task's acceptance prose (tasks.md) still described a PUT-primary writeback path with a fall-back to order-toggle, but ADR-0007 had superseded that: A live probe disconfirmed the PUT/patches path (known 422) and the order-toggle carries the writeback ACs. OQ-A asked whether to also build a parked PUT-and-fall-back stub.
options: Build order-toggle plus a parked PUT-and-fall-back stub to literally match the task's a requirement fallback wording
Build the order-toggle path only
choice: Build the order-toggle path only
rationale: Shipping a PUT path that the build's API is known to reject with 422 would be a dead path — code that can never execute, violating YAGNI and adding a maintenance/confusion surface. ADR-0007 governs and supersedes the stale task prose, and a requirement's literal fallback wording is satisfied historically by the recorded probe. The divergence is a deviation-report item, not a reason to write unreachable code. This is don't-widen-scope: The locked ADR bounds the work to the one path that actually functions.
outcome: The task executor shipped order-toggle only (18/18 unit, gated toggle test with honest status bodies) and the review approved it; the PromptEditor naming divergence and the dropped PUT path were logged for the deviation report.

context: After the cli-shadow fix repaired the demo environment's worker in place, the held a task review could proceed. The review depends on re-running the live signals integration test, which seeds a real item lifecycle (up to ~90s) against the repaired instance.
options: Dispatch the task reviewer and let it run the live signals test as part of review
Run the live signals test in the foreground first, then dispatch the review only if it is green
choice: Run the live signals test in the foreground first, then dispatch the review
rationale: If the live test still failed (e.g., the repair was incomplete), having the reviewer discover it would waste a full review cycle and session budget on a non-code failure. Running it himself first is a cheap pre-check that gates the expensive review dispatch — cost-asymmetry. It also yields fresh green evidence (with logged latencies) that the model could hand to the reviewer, strengthening the verdict. The model ran an import pass for the new a task class_names first and verified supervisor reachability before the test.
outcome: The foreground run passed (rc=0, printer/stamp ~78-79ms, ordered); the model dispatched the review with the evidence logged, and a task was approved. (The lift signal still opportunistically skipped — a build-specific deviation, disclosed.)

context: The task review returned changes-requested with a sharp major: The burst harness's 'zero drops' check was self-referential — it asserted 1:1 only over events the client saw arrive (11 injected creates vs 9 item.created envelopes received, yet the test passed), and its drain loop exited on momentary queue-empty rather than arrival quiescence. The reviewer's open question was whether AC-007's zero-drops is end-to-end or client-pipeline.
options: Hard-assert zero-drops end-to-end (injected creates must equal received envelopes)
Keep the assertion client-pipeline-only and ignore the upstream gap
Hard-assert client-pipeline integrity but additionally reconcile end-to-end loudly with creates-vs-arrivals accounting
choice: Hard-assert client-pipeline integrity and add loud end-to-end creates-vs-arrivals reconciliation
rationale: The NFR governs the client and the supervisor is a black box per assumption A-1, so a hard end-to-end equality assertion would wrongly fail the client for a server-side projection loss it cannot control. But silently dropping 2-of-11 with no accounting is dishonest and hides real loss. The principled split is to keep the hard guarantee where the client is responsible and surface the end-to-end discrepancy loudly so 'slow trailing event' versus 'supervisor dropped it' becomes distinguishable. This also adds quiescence-based settling so the drain no longer exits prematurely.
outcome: The fix round proved via instrumentation that no events were ever dropped — all 11 creates projected 11 item.created frames; the 11->9 was the drain exiting before two trailing envelopes arrived. The harness now settles to quiescence and hard-asserts end-to-end reconciliation (15->15 exact on the green re-run).

context: The operator asked the model to audit running the datastore processes, shut down leftovers from previous subagents, and explain why the subagents left so many. Mapping processes to instances, the model found multiple unregistered a test environment the datastore servers, plus registered instances r7/r9 whose review evidence was already consumed, plus the load-test environment actively in use by an in-flight fixer, and a server whose largest the datastore process initially looked like the oldest leftover.
options: Kill all old-looking the datastore servers including the registered r7/r9 and the large oldest-looking one
Kill only the unregistered orphan servers and defer cli stop on registered instances until in-flight agents finish
choice: Kill only the unregistered orphans now; defer cli stop on registered r7/r9 until the in-flight agents finish
rationale: The model verified each process against /v0/instances before killing, which revealed the 1.3GB oldest-looking server (task001-instance) was actually the registered the 3D view-demo — a registration check that prevented a wrong kill. Unregistered orphans have no supervisor reconciling them, so killing them carries no respawn risk; registered instances require cli stop against the shared supervisor, and cli lifecycle ops had triggered supervisor respawns before, which would disrupt the task E2E running against that supervisor. Reversible-first and verify-before-acting: Defer the riskier a test environment teardown to when it is safe.
outcome: Killed 5 orphaned the datastore servers plus 1 wedged 10-hour tmux client/server, verified dead; all 6 registered instances and the supervisor stayed running=true. R7/r9 deferred. The model also diagnosed the cleanup gap (no teardown contract in earlier briefs, evidence outliving the agent, safety-guard chilling effect).

context: The task re-review confirmed the round-1 major and minor were fixed in code, but issued changes-requested for two mechanical items: The fixer's 'full teardown verified' claim left two live orphans (the load-test environment the datastore server a stray process the model had earlier spared while it was in use, and a background server session load-test environment server), plus an event-injection script was untracked while the dependent tests were staged.
options: Loop back through a third task-executor round to handle the two process kills and the git-add
Handle the two mechanical items directly as orchestrator and mark a task approved-with-completed-hygiene
choice: Handle the orphan kills and git-add directly; treat a task as approved with orchestrator-completed hygiene
rationale: Both remaining items were purely mechanical (kill two confirmed-identity processes, stage one file) with no code change at stake, so spawning a third executor round would spend a full review cycle and session budget on two process kills. The substantive code review was already clean. The model verified process identity against their paths before killing to avoid touching the task's the acceptance-test environment assets, and staged (not committed) the script since committing stays the operator's call. Don't-widen-scope: A re-gate is for code changes, not host hygiene the orchestrator can finish.
outcome: Both orphans reaped and verified gone; an event-injection script staged; a task marked approved with the hygiene handling disclosed for the final report. This made 16/17 tasks settled.

context: The spec-router returned its report assuming the pre-spec protocol (dated notes appended to ai_docs/the foundation doc) would be performed/coordinated by the spec-requirements-architect as 'step zero' of that route. The orchestrator was about to route to requirements. Fable 5 had to decide who authors the foundation doc edits.
options: Delegate the foundation doc dated notes to the requirements-architect as step zero, per the router's assumption
Perform the foundation doc edits directly before invoking the requirements-architect
choice: Perform the foundation doc edits directly before invoking the requirements-architect.
rationale: The requirements architect should *consume* an updated foundation, not author it — feeding it a doc it just wrote conflates the source-of-truth update with the artifact that depends on it. The edits are small, verifiable, and reversible, so doing them in the orchestrator costs little and keeps the dependency direction clean. This is a routing call about WHERE the doc-edit work belongs, deviating from the router's stated assumption. Keeping the foundation edit upstream of requirements preserves the pre-spec protocol's intent that requirements read a settled foundation.
outcome: Fable 5 made three §2/§7a edits plus a header update to the foundation doc directly, then spawned the requirements architect; the requirements report later cites the updated vendor/cli-agent-script facts (C-12/C-13).

context: The operator was deciding OQ-6 (the GDScript read-allowlist), torn between B1 (strict mirror of the dashboard TS allowlist + parity test) and B5 (generate from OpenAPI, default-allow GETs, hand-curated denylist), saying hand-curation 'seems confusing as you'll do it.' B3 (mirror core + justified the 3D view extensions) had been raised but not eliminated; the operator jumped straight to B1-vs-B5.
options: Frame B1-vs-B5 as the operator posed it and let them pick between those two
Reframe: Name that the curation effort is identical and the real discriminator is failure direction, and flag that B1's one con is curable inside B1 via B3 (not B5)
choice: Reframe on failure direction and surface that B3 cures B1's con without inverting safety.
rationale: The operator's 'who curates' worry was a false discriminator — the list is written once either way; what actually differs is fail-closed (B1: New GET blocked until added) vs fail-open (B5: New GET, incl. Side-effecting GET /agent/{base}/prime, silently allowed until denied). Foundation §9 resolved that client-side allowlisting *is* the monitor-mode enforcement (no upstream read-only token), so monitor mode is a safety guarantee, not a convenience filter — that fact bears decisively and was stated, not asserted. Presenting B3 as the real cure for B1's dead-scene con keeps the operator inside the fail-closed posture instead of letting an avoidable con push them to fail-open. This is a marked-lean taste-surface: The purpose call stays with the operator, but the decision frame is corrected.
outcome: Operator replied 'B1 towards B3 matches our view'; OQ-6 resolved as B3 (fail-closed mirror core + parity test + justified extension set), later formalized as ADR-0017 and implemented in a task with 18/18 parity green.

context: The operator reported the AI-editor not appearing as a plugin in Godot settings after running scripts/start.sh. Fable 5 found start.sh defaults to opening vendor/the industrial-protocol layer (whose addons/ lacks the AI-editor), while the addon is correctly installed and enabled in the engine project file. The operator framed it as a plugin problem.
options: Change start.sh's default project dir to the client so the plugin shows
Leave start.sh's default unchanged, prescribe the documented PROTOCOL_PROJECT_DIR override, and offer a separate client wrapper script
choice: Leave start.sh unchanged; prescribe the env override and offer a dedicated client wrapper.
rationale: It was a which-project problem, not a plugin defect — verified by listing both projects' addons/ rather than asserting. Start.sh is deliberately the industrial-protocol layer-project launcher and its build-fingerprint machinery is shared, so mutating its default would be an instance-fix that risks the shared build path; a dedicated wrapper is the structural fix that leaves the established launcher intact. The override is already documented by the script, making it reversible and low-cost. Don't widen scope into the shared launcher to solve a per-developer convenience need.
outcome: Root cause confirmed (start.sh line 36/147 opens vendor/the industrial-protocol layer); operator agreed and asked for the wrapper, which became scripts/start-client.sh.

context: Fable 5 wrote scripts/start-client.sh setting PROTOCOL_PROJECT_DIR=client so start.sh would open the client project. Before finishing, it checked build-the protocol-comms layer.sh and found that the build script ALSO reads PROTOCOL_PROJECT_DIR and installs compiled GDExtension binaries into that project's addons/ — so a client-dir build would pollute the pure-GDScript client and trigger a rebuild loop on every launch.
options: Keep the simple env-override wrapper (PROTOCOL_PROJECT_DIR=client through both build and launch)
Pass --no-build and apply the client path only at launch, guarding against a --build flag and pointing users to start.sh for engine rebuilds
choice: Use --no-build and apply the client override only at launch, with a guard against --build.
rationale: The env var leaks into the build phase, so the naive wrapper would install the protocol-comms layer binaries the client doesn't need and create a missing-artifact → rebuild → re-pollute cycle — verified by grepping build-the protocol-comms layer.sh's install destination rather than assuming. Separating build from launch (engine already built; wrapper only points Godot at client/) keeps the client tree clean, accepting the documented tradeoff that engine changes need start.sh directly. Picking the option that avoids contaminating the client project is the structural fix over a convenient one-liner that would silently rebuild every run.
outcome: Wrapper rewritten to 1433 bytes with --no-build and a build-flag guard; bash -n syntax-checked OK; operator ran it and the AI-editor MCP tools came online by a task (t103).

context: The task context bundle (rebase carried callers onto WireGate) flagged four ambiguities: Streams admission-vs-send, the gate read-return shape, retiring the Writeback dual-path against a carried test, and the no-bypass-scan exemption mechanism. Each came with a bundle recommendation, and ADR-0016's text already spoke to them.
options: Escalate the four ambiguities to the operator as open design questions
Rule on all four as the orchestrator from ADR-0016's language and the approved intent, then dispatch the executor
choice: Rule on all four from ADR-0016 and dispatch.
rationale: These were design-internal questions whose answers follow from already-approved artifacts (ADR-0016's chokepoint language sanctions admit-only for unbounded SSE; additive read-return is mandatory or the rebase is impossible; the no-bypass check already planned the dual-path retirement). Escalating settled-by-spec details would be blocking reversible work the operator already approved in the design phase. The boundary call: In-scope orchestrator rulings vs operator decisions — these are the former, so they don't widen the operator's queue. Recording the rulings in the executor prompt keeps them auditable.
outcome: Executor completed a task with additive send() return {success,status,headers,body}, admit_read() seam, and Writeback direct path retired; review later required changes only for pre-existing the AI-editor working-tree items, not the four rulings.

context: The task review flagged that the engine project file had its two ABOUTME comment lines stripped and carried an `_mcp_game_helper` runtime autoload from the AI-editor — a C-12 conflict (the AI-editor is dev-tool-only, never a runtime dependency). Both changes predated a task: They came from the operator's own the AI-editor plugin enablement in the editor, and client/addons/ is untracked.
options: Silently remove the _mcp_game_helper autoload to satisfy C-12, alongside restoring the ABOUTME lines
Restore only the provably-safe ABOUTME header, leave the autoload untouched, and surface the C-12 tension to the operator at the commit checkpoint
choice: Restore the ABOUTME lines; leave the autoload; defer the C-12 call to the operator.
rationale: Restoring the stripped ABOUTME header is provably safe and required by a hard rule, so it proceeds. Removing the autoload is different in kind: The operator deliberately installed and enabled the AI-editor, the autoload may be load-bearing for the MCP bridge during scene authoring, and the file is uncommitted — silently deleting their dev-environment wiring could break their setup without consent. This is a taste/setup call that belongs to the operator, not a code defect to autonomously fix; the reversible-first move is to flag it at the commit gate. The autoload creates runtime *presence*, not a code dependency, so C-12's intent isn't yet violated in shipped code.
outcome: ABOUTME header restored and the AI-editor wiring preserved (verified by fix-round executor and re-review at t148); C-12 autoload carried forward as a recorded operator-deferred question through every subsequent progress checkpoint (t151, t210).

context: The task context bundle revealed that the 'carried raises-a-hand signal' referenced by a requirement and ADR-0020 does not actually exist in the codebase — 'carried' was a spec misnomer. The pending-interaction flow needs a raise/clear signal driven by PendingTracker. Fable 5 had to decide whether authoring it counts as in-scope.
options: Treat the missing signal as a scope expansion / spec defect and block for operator input
Author the signal fresh following the existing PanelSignals idioms, since a requirement plainly requires the behavior, and note 'carried' was a misnomer
choice: Author the signal fresh per PanelSignals idioms as in-scope.
rationale: A requirement unambiguously requires a raises-a-hand indication; the only thing wrong is the word 'carried' implying it pre-existed. Building the required behavior using established patterns is fulfilling the approved requirement, not widening scope — the boundary test lands inside, so it's not an operator decision. Blocking on a documentation misnomer when the requirement and the pattern are both clear would stall the keystone task for nothing. Recording the misnomer correction in the done-note keeps the spec honest without re-opening the gate.
outcome: Executor authored the signal + PendingTracker + ApprovalSlip; review approved a task (40/40 unit green, live respond green), with a separate major advisory carried for the untestable APPLIED-latch branch.

context: A reviewer advisory asked a task to add a third out-of-scope 'services' silent-drop vector to match AC-010/a requirement, which name external-message.*, provider.*, and services. The follow-up executor blocked: 'services' is a REST snapshot resource (GET /services per foundation §7a), not a stream event type — verified absent from vendor events.go KnownEventTypes — so AC-010 mis-describes the wire. Fixing it means amending a user-approved artifact.
options: Pick a resolution autonomously and amend the approved AC (e.g. Truth-fix + substitute a real provider.* vector)
Stop and ask the operator via AskUserQuestion which way to amend the approved requirement
choice: Stop and ask the operator which way to resolve the AC-010 inconsistency.
rationale: The executor was right to block rather than guess (per hard rules), and the underlying facts were verified against events.go and foundation §7a, not asserted. But editing a user-approved requirements artifact is a genuine scope/spec decision that belongs to the operator — quietly rewriting an AC the operator signed off on would erode the approval gate's meaning. Presenting three concrete options with a marked recommended lean (truth-fix + real third vector) optimizes the operator's discrimination step while leaving the call theirs. Verify-before-asserting plus defer-the-purpose-call: Confirm the inconsistency is real, then hand the amend decision up.
outcome: Operator chose the recommended truth-fix + real third vector; a follow-up executor amended AC-010/a requirement, swapped in provider.health_gate_alert, and re-verified 40/40 green (t246).

context: A task (acceptance E2E) review found the AC-009 batch leg structurally non-firing: The test called _harness.dispatch() (which creates+closes the batch) BEFORE launch, so batch events folded into the snapshot baseline (SeqCursor.ABSENT) and could never project as signals through the event stream — the 150s wait window was irrelevant. The done-note had described it as an environment-gated LOUD-SKIP.
options: Accept the batch leg as an environment-gated skip and mark AC-009 satisfied via the task's unit suite
Loop back to the executor to reorder batch create/close to AFTER launch with taps installed, fixing the root cause
choice: Reorder the batch production to post-launch and fix the root cause.
rationale: The reviewer's diagnosis was dispositive and the skip was a structural defect, not an environment gate — calling it environment-gated in the done-note was inaccurate. The task contract specifies AC-009 is only satisfiable through the task's live E2E (with a narrow fallback), so accepting a unit-suite substitute would hollow out the acceptance. Fixing ordering so events flow through envelope_applied is the structural fix that makes the assertion real, versus papering over it with a skip. The changes-requested verdict correctly loops to the executor rather than the orchestrator hand-editing the test.
outcome: Executor moved batch create/close after launch+taps; re-review approved a task with the batch leg now firing and the done-note root-cause corrected (t292), latencies 184ms/177ms.

context: For a task (route startup through SceneDiff so DiffApplier spawns every tower/floor/lane), the Snapshot record has no rigs field and the loader does not read /rigs, yet the spec requires one tower per rig. DesiredSet.add_agent contributes lane and pool-floor nodes but no rig nodes, so with an empty rigs array there are no rig keys and no towers. The task builder had grouped agents by their rig attribution to get towers.
options: Add a /rigs read to the snapshot loader so rig keys come from the wire
Derive rig nodes from agent rig-attribution inside DesiredSet.add_agent (ensure a rig:<name> node when an agent carries rig attribution)
choice: Derive rig nodes from agent attribution in DesiredSet.add_agent; leave the loader untouched.
rationale: Aligns with ADR-0009's single-record-derivability principle — every node key is a function of one wire dict, and rig attribution already rides on the agent dict, so no new fetch is needed. Keeps the code path unified through the one diff engine (ADR-0008) rather than adding a parallel /rigs read that the loader would otherwise never use, which matters for a task reconcile consistency. Smallest-change: A minimal {"name": Rig} node that SceneLayout._scope already handles, versus a new loader endpoint and Snapshot field. Side benefit: Even a dispatcher-only rig surfaces a tower because the dispatcher carries rig attribution.
outcome: A task approved; startup routes through the unified diff engine at 335ms with towers materializing from attribution. A separate despawn-registry-cascade hazard was flagged for a task.

context: For a task live churn, AC-005 requires a session.woke event to introduce a wholly-unseen agent into the scene, but the session.woke envelope carries only the Subject display name — no pool or rig attribution. A solo lane keyed by Subject could later mismatch the qualified-name key that reconcile produces with full attribution, triggering a despawn-and-respawn under ADR-0009's rename semantics.
options: Spawn an instance-scoped solo lane keyed by Subject immediately, refining placement at reconcile (a task)
Restrict animations to agents already in the scene and defer all new spawns to reconcile
Fetch the agent's full detail via a targeted GET to recover pool attribution before spawning
choice: Spawn an instance-scoped solo lane keyed by Subject now; refine placement at reconcile.
rationale: Option 2 fails AC-005's literal requirement that a woke event introduce the agent live. The targeted detail-GET would recover attribution but reintroduces a poll on the live-stream path, conflicting with the carried no-re-poll discipline, and opens new ADR-0001 questions. ADR-0009 explicitly accepts solo-lane re-key as despawn-old + spawn-new, so the later key mismatch is an already-sanctioned transition, not a bug — making option 1 the reversible-first choice that keeps the event path clean. The placement refinement is bounded and routed to a task rather than blocking now.
outcome: A task approved and working end-to-end; a minor phantom-session-spawn-log note was carried to a task. Later eyes-on run confirmed two lanes spawned live via the churn diff from session.woke in ~2ms each.

context: A task fully widened the InScopeEvent Kind enum to 34 members while the total-function signal-coverage gate (AC-009) requires every in-scope Kind to have a registered signal. The signal registrations live in the task's files, which the orchestrator's own no-cross-task-edits discipline forbids touching during a task. So a fully-widened enum necessarily leaves the coverage gate red — 25 PASS / 1 FAIL — but the task's done-criteria says existing suites must stay green.
options: Ship a task with the suite red, framing the designed red window explicitly to the reviewer and running a task immediately after to restore green
Partially widen the enum or touch the task's files to keep the gate green within a task
choice: Ship the designed red window to review with explicit framing, then run a task immediately to restore green.
rationale: The constraint conflict is structural: A total gate over a fully-widened enum cannot be green until the registrations exist, and those belong to a task by decomposition. Touching the task's files inside a task would violate the task-boundary discipline and muddy review attribution. Naming the red window to the reviewer turns an apparent regression into a declared, time-boxed handoff the reviewer can adjudicate, rather than hiding it. The red window is bounded to a single task transition and closed deterministically by the very next task, so the cost is contained.
outcome: A task approved conditional on a task restoring green; a task then closed the window at 26/26 PASS, zero FAIL.

context: A task must deliver a mail.sent message-transfer animation. The visual tube can either run lane-local through the signal pipeline, or route into the InfraFixtures dispatch the message log fixture's slot. ADR-0015 and the infra-fixtures module code comment say tubes travel through the dispatch the message log, but the main scene script and InfraFixtures are not listed in the task's Files section, raising the question of whether this routing belongs to the task at all — and whether it is a call only the operator should make.
options: Defer the tube-routing decision to the operator as a purpose call
Implement fixture-routed delivery from the main scene script's envelope handler per ADR-0015, treating the Files-list omission as a decomposition gap
Route the tube lane-local through the signal pipeline, threading from/to fields through it
choice: Implement fixture-routed delivery from the main scene script's envelope handler per ADR-0015; do not defer to the operator.
rationale: The model judged this a technical implementation detail already settled by a user-approved ADR (ADR-0015) and a code comment naming a task as the owner, not an open purpose call — so escalating would be redundant. The lane-local route would have to thread from/to through the signal pipeline; the fixture route reuses the main scene script's existing full-envelope access for a simpler, parallel path that leaves SIGNAL_TRANSPORT and its <2s seam unchanged. The Files-list omission was read as a decomposition oversight rather than evidence against the routing, since the design consistently points to the dispatch fixture. Deterministic lexicographic agent→slot mapping kept it aligned with SceneLayout's no-hand-placement principle.
outcome: A task approved after the reviewer ran it independently (14/18); mail dedup + message-transfer delivery verified. Later eyes-on run showed mail flying into town-crier's slot.

context: The task acceptance E2E ran live and passed (52/0), but the orchestrator noticed the executor had filed an issue rather than patching a production defect: The main scene script._load_orders reads the items key while the live /orders endpoint returns {"orders": [...]}, so the real the status board can never populate against a live supervisor. The AC-008 the status board-toggle leg had nonetheless reported green — apparently because it exercised the Writeback API directly rather than the live the status board binding, routing around the defect.
options: Accept the executor's filed issue and defer the fix to post-slice backlog
Rule the one-field fix in scope (a requirement/ADR-0012), send it back to the executor, and treat AC-008's prior green as suspect until re-verified through the production path
choice: Rule the fix in scope, send it back to the executor, and re-verify AC-008 through the real the status board path.
rationale: Verify-before-asserting applied to a green test: The model interrogated *why* AC-008 passed and found the E2E had bypassed the very binding the requirement covers, so the green was not load-bearing. A defect that makes the production the status board permanently empty is a correctness failure against a requirement/ADR-0012, not an out-of-scope nicety, and the fix is a single field name — too cheap and too central to defer. Deferring would ship a slice whose headline fixture is silently broken in production behind a test that lied. The E2E's value was precisely exposing what the unit/integration path routed around.
outcome: Fix applied (orders-first read with items fallback); live E2E re-ran green with the production the status board genuinely populating; re-review confirmed _load_orders reads orders-first at line 610. The bug and fix are recorded in the final commit body.

context: The task executor reported two questionable things in the regression seal: It split the intended single 'run all gated suites as one block' into three instance-shape families (claiming the one-block approach was infeasible), and it reported that cli init --from on the shared supervisor reconciles and cycles the demo environment — a protected instance that constraint C-9 forbids writing to or restarting.
options: Accept the executor's report and close the final task
Independently verify the test environment claim (whether the demo environment was actually restarted/written) and scrutinize the split before sign-off
choice: Independently verify both the test environment claim and the split before closing the slice.
rationale: A possible breach of the C-9 a test environment invariant is exactly the class of claim an orchestrator must not take on faith at sign-off — verify-before-asserting, with the asymmetry that an unverified breach corrupts the shared environment other work depends on. Routing the verification through a code-reviewer subagent gets an independent read on both the demo environment cycling and the instance-shape-family split rather than the executor self-certifying its own questionable calls. The final task is the last gate before the slice is declared done, so latent hygiene or invariant violations are most expensive to miss here.
outcome: Reviewer found the demo environment's the datastore process had stayed alive ~3 hours (the supervisor restarted, not the instance; protected data intact) and surfaced six leaked scratch the datastore servers, which were then verified and reaped; the infeasible one-block run was recorded as a structural finding. A task approved after the fix.

context: Asked to commit the slice, the orchestrator found that during a task it had added !coverage negation lines to the root .gitignore to un-exclude the coverage-gate test and ADR-0013. But git ls-files / check-ignore show the root .gitignore is deliberately self-ignored (its own line 4) and has never been tracked — so a fresh clone would not have the file or its negations at all. The two previously-excluded files are already staged.
options: Force-add the root .gitignore so the negation lines are tracked and survive a clone
Leave the .gitignore change local-only and rely on the two files being staged directly
choice: Leave the .gitignore change local-only; commit the two un-ignored files directly.
rationale: Force-adding would override a deliberate repo policy (the file self-ignores by design) for no real benefit. The actual goal — the coverage test and ADR-0013 surviving a fresh clone — is already met by staging those two files explicitly, since a fresh clone has no root .gitignore to exclude anything in the first place. Reversible-first / don't-fight-the-repo: Honoring the existing self-ignore convention avoids a policy violation that would only matter on the local working copy. The model traced the chain to the concrete clone outcome rather than reflexively 'fixing' the ignore file.
outcome: Slice committed as f9bcb23 on master (107 files, +17,748 / −178), working tree clean; the .gitignore negations stayed local, recorded as a harmless caveat in project memory.

context: The operator asked for a test 'we can see with our eyes.' A real X11 display is available (2560×1440). The model needs an instance to render: The demo environment is small, non-destructive and already live, or a fresh scratch instance could be materialized from the committed the live-activity scenario fixture. The shared supervisor on 8372 also hosts the protected the demo environment.
options: Render against the existing read-only the demo environment for the eyes-on run
Materialize a fresh scratch instance (the visual-check environment) from fixtures/the live-activity scenario, render it, then tear it down
choice: Materialize a fresh scratch the visual-check environment from the live-activity scenario fixture, render it windowed, then tear down.
rationale: The eyes-on run involves write activity (sending mail, injecting items to drive churn animations) that would mutate state, so it must not touch the protected the demo environment — isolation on a disposable scratch instance keeps the demonstration non-destructive per the standing C-9 discipline. The live-activity scenario fixture also shows two rigs and the full infra fixtures, exercising the actual slice that was just built, whereas the demo environment is a thinner the single-lane era topology. The model verified DISPLAY availability first before committing to a windowed launch, and pre-planned teardown so the scratch resource would not leak.
outcome: The visual-check environment rendered in a real window (2 rigs, 9 agents); mail flew into town-crier's slot and 6 injected items woke lanes live via the churn diff. Afterward the visual-check environment was torn down (404, scratch dir gone, no stray the datastore servers), the demo environment untouched.

context: All 17 tasks were settled and the operator had explicitly opted into a verification workflow producing an implemented-vs-spec report. The model had to design how to verify each of the eight plan parts (wire, scene-gen, signals, writeback, fixture, skeleton, NFR, acceptance) plus run the test suite. The shared Godot engine uses an import cache that collides if multiple headless runs execute concurrently.
options: Run all 8-9 verifiers free to execute tests in parallel
Make verifiers read-only and confine test execution to a single dedicated suite runner (plus a completeness critic as a barrier)
Verify serially, one part at a time
choice: Make verifiers read-only and confine test execution to a single dedicated suite runner, with a completeness critic barrier.
rationale: Eight read-only verifiers can safely run in parallel because they only read code/spec and emit AC-level verdicts; the hazard is concurrent Godot test runs colliding on the import cache, so isolating execution to one suite runner removes the only shared-state conflict (don't-widen-scope of the conflict surface). A single critic barrier consolidates the deviation list and cross-checks every REQ/AC for coverage gaps, which a swarm of independent verifiers cannot do alone. This maximizes parallel throughput while keeping the one mutating activity serialized.
outcome: Workflow wzhajshi2 ran 10 agents (~950k tokens) and completed; produced per-part verdicts, a suite result (exit=1, 1 fail), and a critic consolidation of 20 deviations and unverified a requirement.

context: The operator granted commit authority and asked the model to tackle the needs-attention list (13 items in the report). The model had previously used parallel task-executor subagents for the build. Several fixes touch overlapping files (the main scene script, tasks.md, run_tests.sh, multiple test files).
options: Spawn parallel task-executor subagents per needs-attention item
Handle the edits directly and sequentially in the main thread
choice: Handle the edits directly and sequentially rather than spawning parallel task-executors.
rationale: The standing directive permits parallelism only when work does not clash, and these fixes are manageable in size while several touch the same files (the main scene script, tasks.md, run_tests.sh) — parallel agents would collide. Direct sequential editing keeps the file-contention risk at zero and preserves a single coherent ordering for the eventual commit. The cost of serial execution is acceptable because no single item is large.
outcome: All eight tracked tasks completed sequentially and landed in commit d45e104 (17 files, +598/-62) with the full suite green 21/21.

context: The operator stated '.gitignore and settings.json are repaired' and instructed the model to proceed to committing. Before committing, the model ran git check-ignore on the fixture's agent.toml and found a second .gitignore block at lines 200-201 still excluding the fixture files and the root .gitignore itself.
options: Trust the operator's 'repaired' assertion and commit as-is
Verify the repair with check-ignore and fix the remaining exclusion block before committing
choice: Verify with check-ignore, find the incomplete repair, and remove the harmful lines 200-201 before committing.
rationale: Verify-before-asserting: The operator's claim that the fixture was trackable was checkable cheaply with git check-ignore, and the check disconfirmed it — line 200 (bare .gitignore) would prevent committing the root ignore file and line 201 duplicated the scoped /agents/ rule, still hiding the fixture's only agent definition. Committing on the unverified claim would have produced a broken fixture in version control, the exact highest-priority finding from the report. The fix was a scoped two-line deletion aligned with the operator's evident intent, not a scope expansion.
outcome: Removed lines 200-201; check-ignore then confirmed agent.toml and fixtures/the demo environment/.gitignore were no longer ignored; both files staged and committed in d279143 (112 files, +13059).

context: Staging the repo's first-ever commit. The model observed the repo was on branch 'master' while the environment's configured primary branch is 'main'. The operator had said to proceed committing.
options: Rename the branch to main before the root commit to match the environment expectation
Commit on the existing master branch and keep moving
choice: Commit on the current master branch without renaming.
rationale: Reversible-first and don't-widen-scope: The branch name is trivially changeable later, this is the root commit so no history is disrupted, and the operator explicitly said to proceed. Renaming would add a step outside the requested work for a cosmetic mismatch. The model noted the discrepancy explicitly rather than silently ignoring it.
outcome: Root commit d279143 landed on master; the master/main mismatch was carried forward and never blocked subsequent commits (d45e104, 20458b7, 9ccf940 all on master).

context: Implementing the task 'config-selectable' transport knob (the missing capability flagged by verification). The model typed the event source variable as the base EventSource and needed to call configure() through it, but configure() existed only on the concrete SseEventSource/LongPollEventSource, not the base. GDScript strict typing would reject calling an undeclared method on the base type.
options: Keep the concrete type or cast at the call site to reach configure()
Add configure() as an abstract method to the EventSource base class to complete the interface
choice: Add configure() to the EventSource base class to complete the shared interface.
rationale: Structural-fix vs instance-fix: The real defect is that the transport seam's contract (configure/start/stop) was incomplete on the base, so a cast would paper over a genuine interface gap and leave the next transport-swap caller stuck. Declaring configure() abstract on the base lets SceneMain hold an EventSource-typed field and select transports at construction without per-call casts, which is the whole point of the config-selectable design. It also let the model fix the now-false 'future transport' comment in the same pass.
outcome: Create_event_source() static factory + CLI_TRANSPORT knob shipped; the transport-selection test passed (sse/longpoll/unknown->loud SSE fallback); the preload also proved the main scene script parses.

context: Writing the first unit test for PromptEditor (zero prior coverage). The model needed to test commit() -> committed-signal -> Writeback.toggle_order. CLAUDE.md has a hard no-mock rule. The project's existing the write-back test tests failure paths against a real Writeback pointed at an unreachable port (localhost:9), and a real-supervisor success path lives in integration tests.
options: Stub Writeback with an inner subclass that records calls and returns a canned Dictionary
Use a real Writeback against an unreachable port for the failure path (unit) and route the existing integration test's toggle through PromptEditor.commit() for the success path
choice: Use a real Writeback against an unreachable port for unit failure coverage and route the integration test's toggle through PromptEditor for the success path.
rationale: The no-mock rule forbids stubbing the collaborator, and the project already had an established real-data pattern (unreachable port for failure edges) to follow rather than inventing a mock. Routing the live integration toggle through PromptEditor.commit() covers the success path against a real supervisor and simultaneously makes the panel's own comment ('integration suite connects to observe the outcome') true rather than aspirational. This is the minimal change that satisfies both the rule and full coverage without fabricated test doubles.
outcome: The prompt-editor test passed (committed names the order, reports success=false, exactly one loud ClientErrorChannel report); a panel-driven leg added to the write-back-action test; both registered and green.

context: Adding unit assertions for SSE reconnect mechanics (backoff schedule + Last-Event-ID header on reconnect), a flagged gap. The relevant state (_backoff_ms, _reconnect_at_ms, the resume header) lives in private fields of SseEventSource, and the existing test suite follows a black-box / no-internal-poke convention.
options: Reach into private fields (_backoff_ms, _reconnect_at_ms) to assert the schedule directly
Stand up an in-process Godot TCPServer on an ephemeral port and assert reconnect timing and the Last-Event-ID header from the real request bytes at the network boundary
choice: Use an in-process TCPServer at the network boundary to observe real request bytes and reconnect timing.
rationale: Testing at the network seam preserves the project's black-box philosophy and asserts the externally observable contract (header present/absent, gap between reconnects roughly doubling) rather than implementation details that could change. Reading raw request bytes confirms Last-Event-ID is sent with the resume key on connect and reconnect, and omitted at SeqCursor.ABSENT, with no mocks. The trade-off — loose timing tolerances to avoid headless flakiness — is acceptable because the schedule shape, not exact milliseconds, is what matters.
outcome: Reconnect sub-tests passed with measured gaps 1007ms then 2008ms confirming the 1s->2s backoff; Last-Event-ID present on connect/reconnect and absent at ABSENT.

context: During the full suite run the live-signals test failed an ordering assertion: The supervisor projected item.created at a higher seq than item.closed for the same item (a realtime-hook vs reconcile race), reproduced roughly every other run. The client played the signals in seq order exactly as designed. The acceptance E2E carried the same printer-seq <= stamp-seq assertion and could false-fail identically.
options: Treat the failure as a client defect and try to force causal created-before-closed ordering
Relax both tests to assert only the client's actual guarantee (monotonic seq-ordered playout) and loudly NOTE the supervisor anomaly instead of false-failing
Leave the assertion and accept an intermittently red suite
choice: Relax both tests to the client's real guarantee (monotonic playout) and NOTE the supervisor anomaly.
rationale: The assertion encoded a false premise — that the supervisor preserves causal lifecycle order — which empirical runs disconfirmed; the client only ever guarantees non-decreasing seq playout, so the test should assert what is true, not what was hoped. This is a structural fix to the verification contract, not a patch to one run: Both live-signals and the acceptance E2E carried the same brittle assertion, so both were corrected together. Loudly noting the anomaly preserves the finding for triage rather than hiding it behind a green check.
outcome: Relaxed assertion passed across 147 signal starts on a run where the race recurred (created 69840 after closed 69834, then 70350 after 70345); both files updated; documented as triaged in the implementation report §2.
