context: The bounded live observation had measured the worker-marker resume (re-enable) pickup lag at 45-105 seconds, far exceeding the 10s cooldown. The suite's writeback transition test used a 25s RESUME window and hard-failed because the cadence had not resumed within it. This is a test-assumption mismatch with real supervisor scheduler behavior, not a client bug.
options: Leave the 25s window and accept the transition leg failing or skipping
Widen the RESUME window to ~150s and add an early-exit-on-first-marker parameter so a healthy instance returns fast while a genuinely dead one still proves absence
choice: Widen the RESUME window to 150s with a first-marker early-exit parameter.
rationale: The measured scheduler pickup lag (45-105s) is ground truth, so the test's window was simply wrong and had to match observed reality. Adding early_exit_count=1 keeps the common healthy case cheap (returns on the first marker) while preserving the full-window proof for the STOP leg where absence must be demonstrated — avoiding a routine multi-minute cost on every gated run. The same too-tight window existed in the acceptance E2E, so the fix was applied there too rather than only to the failing instance.
outcome: Test_writeback_next_action hard-passed the complete STOP/RESUME transition (14 assertions); a requirement — the one Must that had never passed live — became suite-asserted; acceptance E2E patched identically.

context: The operator asked whether three apparently-leftover the datastore processes could be shut down. The model found only one persistent the datastore sql-server (the demo environment, load-bearing for every supervisor-gated test); the other two were transient children respawning every ~10s because the re-enabled worker-marker order fires continuously, writing ~8.6k DEMO-MARKER items/day and measurably growing the datastore store.
options: Unilaterally disable the worker-marker order to quiet the churn
Keep it firing
Explain that only one process is load-bearing, surface the accumulation trade-off, and ask the operator whether to disable the marker
choice: Explain that nothing is a true leftover, surface the item-accumulation trade-off, and ask before disabling.
rationale: Disabling the marker is a state change to a shared/protected instance with a real cost-benefit the operator owns — it quiets churn and store growth but the transition tests re-enable it anyway, so the call is about durable rest state, a purpose decision. The honest finding (one persistent process, two transient) corrected the operator's premise of 'three leftovers' rather than acting on it. Presenting the exact disable command with a marked recommendation defers the taste call while making it one-click for the operator.
outcome: Operator replied 'if it's not useful then you can disable/shutdown it' and asked the model to proceed to the two open items; the model then disabled the marker.

context: Closing report item 11: Two live transport tests (the live SSE-source test, the long-poll-path test) converted a quiet instance into SKIP-style vacuous passes — when no traffic arrived they passed without asserting anything. With worker-marker now disabled the instance is reliably quiet, making the soft-pass the normal outcome.
options: Keep tolerating quiet windows (leave the soft-pass backstop)
Have each test self-inject one real item via an event-injection script after opening the source, and hard-assert envelope arrival so an empty window is a FAIL
choice: Self-inject one real item per test and hard-assert arrival, turning a quiet window into a failure.
rationale: A test that passes when it observes nothing verifies nothing; the structural fix is to guarantee the traffic the test depends on rather than hope the instance produces it. An event-injection script issues a genuine svc create (no mock, honoring the hard rule), so arrival becomes a real assertion. Open-before-inject ordering ensures the envelope lands on the already-open stream/poll, and the same pattern closes both legs of the long-poll exclusive-resume check cheaply instead of burning ~140s of empty retries.
outcome: Both tests passed green on the fully quiet instance; long-poll proved both legs with real seqs (live poll 74146-74147, exclusive resume 74148-74151, no replay); item 11 marked RESOLVED in the report.

context: The full suite run revealed that test_writeback_next_action's cleanup always re-enabled worker-marker ('restore a test environment state'), which silently undid the operator's just-approved disable on every gated run, and the 25s baseline window could not see the 45-105s pickup lag from a disabled start, so the transition leg silently skipped. The supervisor's enabled flag does not flip on GET-back on this build, so the test cannot read and restore the true prior state.
options: Leave cleanup re-enabling worker-marker (always-on rest state) and accept the silent skip
Change cleanup to restore DISABLED as the agreed rest state and widen the baseline leg to the same 150s early-exit window
choice: Make cleanup restore the DISABLED rest state and widen the baseline window to match the resume leg.
rationale: The operator's decision made disabled the durable rest state, so a cleanup that re-enables on every run contradicts that intent and resumes ~8.6k items/day of store growth — a one-line policy correction with an explanatory comment is in scope of the disable directive. Because the build's GET-back enabled flag is unreliable, exact prior-state restoration is impossible, so encoding the agreed rest state is the honest choice over pretending to round-trip. The baseline leg waits on the identical enable-pickup lag as the resume leg, so reusing the 150s early-exit window keeps the transition assertion active instead of reintroducing a routine soft-skip.
outcome: Renamed _restore_enabled -> _restore_rest_state (disable); re-ran and the full transition hard-passed from the disabled rest state (baseline->stopped->resumed, no skip) with cleanup leaving the order disabled; committed in 20458b7.

context: The operator asked the model to read the foundation doc, assess what the single-lane slice achieved, and formulate a new /spec prompt for a fresh session. Foundation §8 lists several deferred directions (multi-agent macro the 3D view, infrastructure fixtures, monitor mode, hardware twins). The MVP also surfaced a snapshot-pagination gap that blocks any busy instance.
options: Ask the operator which deferred direction to scope next

Recommend a specific slice-2 scope (the live-activity scenario: Multi-lane, macro the 3D view, auto-layout, agent churn, reconnect/reconcile, expanded event coverage) with a marked lean and brief alternatives, keeping monitor-mode and hardware twins deferred
choice: Recommend the live-activity scenario scale-out slice with a marked lean, rather than asking which direction to take.
rationale: The operator delegated the formulation, so presenting a reasoned recommendation respects that more than bouncing the decision back. The lean is grounded in structure, not taste alone: Live churn and post-reconnect reconcile share one scene-diff engine, and snapshot pagination already blocks busy instances, so those naturally cluster into slice 2 while monitor-mode and hardware twins stay deferred per foundation's own priority. Noting the alternatives keeps the purpose call legible to the operator without abdicating the synthesis that was requested.
outcome: Operator accepted the direction and proceeded ('yes add the dated notes'); the /spec the live-activity scenario prompt was delivered and the foundation got five dated 2026-06-10 notes committed in 9ccf940.

context: The worker-marker baseline had finally fired during this session (the first time across many attempts), making the requirement stop/resume transition — never previously demonstrated live — exercisable. The model had to decide how to validate it before claiming it passed. The disable returned an honest {"status":"disabled"} but the build's GET-back enabled flag is known unreliable.
options: Trust the disable/enable HTTP 200 responses and mark a requirement verified
Run a bounded three-leg observation (45s enabled baseline, 45s disabled STOP leg, then RESUME leg) counting actual markers above an X-CLI-Index seq floor
choice: Run the bounded three-leg live observation against the event stream rather than trusting the HTTP responses.
rationale: Verify-before-asserting: Because the supervisor's GET-back enabled flag does not reflect reality on this build, the only trustworthy evidence is the observable cadence on the event stream — markers above a captured seq floor. Counting 4 markers/45s enabled, 0/45s disabled, and resumption after re-enable is a falsifiable demonstration the HTTP status codes alone cannot provide. When the first 45s RESUME window showed zero, the model extended to 105s rather than concluding failure, since the unknown scheduler pickup lag was the more likely explanation.
outcome: Baseline 4 markers, disabled 0, re-enable resumed at 6 markers by 105s; transition demonstrated end-to-end and documented in tasks.md and the report; exposed the 45-105s pickup lag that later drove the window-widening fix.

context: Working item 3 of the implementation-report needs-attention list (the task 'config-selectable transport' gap), the client gained a CLI_TRANSPORT env knob and a SceneMain.create_event_source() static factory. The factory must decide what to do when the configured transport string is unrecognized.
options: Silently fall back to a default transport on an unknown CLI_TRANSPORT value
Map unknown -> a loud SSE fallback that surfaces the bad config while still running
choice: Map unknown -> a loud SSE fallback that surfaces the bad config while still running
rationale: A silent default would hide operator misconfiguration of CLI_TRANSPORT, letting a typo pass unnoticed; routing an unknown value to a loud SSE fallback keeps the client running on a safe default while making the misconfiguration visible. The factory was made the single composition-root decision point so transport selection is testable in isolation rather than threaded through scene wiring. Dedicated the transport-selection test covers sse/longpoll/unknown branches.
outcome: CLI_TRANSPORT knob plus create_event_source() factory and the transport-selection test added; full suite green 21/21, exit 0.

context: The live-signals test and the acceptance E2E originally asserted the order in which the supervisor causally projects events. Live observation reproduced item.created seq 70350 projecting after item.closed 70345, showing the supervisor's causal projection order is empirically racy and not a reliable assertion target.
options: Continue asserting the supervisor causal projection order (empirically racy; produces false failures)
Assert the client's own guarantee — monotonic playout seqs — and note the upstream anomaly loudly without failing
choice: Assert the client's own guarantee — monotonic playout seqs — and note the upstream anomaly loudly without failing
rationale: A test should assert the guarantee the client itself owns, not a racy upstream projection ordering it cannot control; asserting monotonic playout seqs tests the pipeline's actual contract. The observed reordering (item.created 70350 after item.closed 70345) was reproduced live, so asserting projection order would yield non-deterministic false failures. The anomaly is logged loudly so it is never silently swallowed, but it never false-fails the suite. This trades a flaky assertion for a stable one without losing visibility.
outcome: The live-signals test and acceptance E2E re-pointed to monotonic playout seqs; reordering anomaly noted loudly, never false-fails; full suite green 21/21, exit 0.

context: A requirement (writeback stop/resume transition) had only manual observation. The existing transition suites used a 25s RESUME window; the live scheduler pickup lag for re-enable was measured at 45-105s, exceeding the 10s cooldown, so a 25s window never actually observed the transition and the test could pass without exercising it.
options: Keep the 25s RESUME window (shorter than the measured 45-105s pickup lag, so the transition is skipped/vacuously passed)
Widen RESUME windows to 150s with a first-marker early-exit so the full stop/resume transition is hard-asserted on the demo environment
choice: Widen RESUME windows to 150s with a first-marker early-exit so the full stop/resume transition is hard-asserted on the demo environment
rationale: A test window shorter than the measured scheduler pickup lag (45-105s) lets the assertion window close before the transition can be observed, so the test passes without actually verifying a requirement. Widening the window to 150s makes the empty/transition case observable, and the first-marker early-exit keeps the test fast when the transition does arrive promptly. The manual bounded observation (baseline 4 markers/45s -> disabled 0 markers/45s -> resumed) grounds the window sizing in measured behavior rather than a guess. This converts a vacuous pass into a real assertion.
outcome: Both transition suites widened 25s->150s; test_writeback_next_action HARD-PASSED the full stop/resume transition on the demo environment; resume lag measured 45-105s vs the 10s cooldown.

context: Report item 11 concerned the idle scenario SKIP-style-pass window: The live SSE-source test and the long-poll-path test attached at head, so on a quiet instance they could see no events and pass vacuously without proving the transport actually delivers anything.
options: Leave the tests dependent on ambient instance traffic (an empty window passes vacuously)
Have each test guarantee its own traffic — open-before-inject, create one real item via an event-injection script, and HARD-assert envelope arrival so an empty window FAILS
choice: Have each test guarantee its own traffic — open-before-inject, create one real item via an event-injection script, and HARD-assert envelope arrival so an empty window FAILS
rationale: A start-at-head attach only sees events projected after it, so a quiet instance yields an empty window that a SKIP-style test treats as success; an empty test window must FAIL rather than vacuously pass. Opening the source before injecting and then creating exactly one real item (genuine svc create, 1:1 with item.created) gives the test deterministic traffic it controls. Hard-asserting envelope arrival means the absence of the event is now a failure signal. This removes the dependence on ambient instance activity entirely.
outcome: Verified on a fully quiet instance — sse-live green; longpoll-path green on both legs (live poll seqs 74146-74147, exclusive resume 74148-74151, no replay); full suite exit 0.

context: Item 3 had a sub-finding: No test drove the long-poll fallback transport all the way into the signal pipeline, so AC-011's signal clause was only proven on SSE. The long-poll path's end-to-end signal behavior on the fallback transport was unverified.
options: Leave the long-poll fallback unverified end-to-end (signal clause proven on SSE only)
Add the long-poll signals test that forces CLI_TRANSPORT=longpoll on the real the main scene and asserts a signal starts over the long-poll transport
choice: Add the long-poll signals test that forces CLI_TRANSPORT=longpoll on the real the main scene and asserts a signal starts over the long-poll transport
rationale: Proving the signal clause only on SSE leaves the fallback transport's end-to-end behavior assumed rather than verified; instancing the real composition root with CLI_TRANSPORT=longpoll confirms the root actually selects LongPollEventSource and that an injected item drives a signal over that transport. Exercising the production the main scene (not a stub) makes the test cover the real wiring path. The latency and monotonicity are measured to bound the claim concretely.
outcome: The long-poll signals test asserts LongPollEventSource selected, observes the printer (item.created) signal over long-poll at 10 ms event->signal latency (< 2000 ms), playout seqs monotonic; AC-011 signal clause holds end-to-end on the fallback; registered in the supervisor-gated suite.

context: The worker-marker standing order ran on a 10s cadence. The writeback test cleanup previously always re-enabled the order on exit, leaving it running in a rest state. At a 10s cadence the order grows the datastore store by roughly 8,600 items/day, and nothing consumes those items.
options: Keep re-enabling the order on test exit (10s cadence rest state grows the store ~8.6k items/day with no consumer)
Invert the rest state — cleanup DISABLES the order on every exit; the baseline leg's pre-test enable then rides the same measured 45-105s pickup lag as resume
choice: Invert the rest state — cleanup DISABLES the order on every exit; the baseline leg's pre-test enable then rides the same measured 45-105s pickup lag as resume
rationale: This is a cost-asymmetry call flagged as an operator decision: An always-on 10s cadence accumulates ~8.6k items/day in the store with no consumer, a real storage cost with no benefit, so the safe rest state is disabled. A bonus is test fidelity — from a disabled rest state the pre-test enable incurs the same measured 45-105s scheduler pickup lag as the resume leg, whereas the old 25s baseline window always skipped the transition. Disabling on every exit makes the cost-free state the default after any run. The wide 150s pickup window with first-marker early-exit keeps the baseline leg both correct and fast.
outcome: Cleanup now disables on exit; baseline uses the 150s pickup window; verified green with the FULL stop/resume transition HARD-asserted from the disabled rest state (75840 -> 75928 -> 75980); suite exit 0.

context: Slice 2 (the live-activity scenario) must build the 3D the 3D view at startup, mutate it under live churn (spawn/despawn of towers/floors/lanes), and rebuild it on reconnect/reconcile. Each of these three scenarios could be implemented with its own bespoke node-mutation path, risking ghost or duplicate nodes where the paths disagree.
options: Three separate code paths for startup, live churn, and reconcile node mutation
One diff engine — SceneDiff over stable NodeIdentity keys — serving startup, live churn, and reconcile alike (ADR-0008/0009)
choice: One diff engine — SceneDiff over stable NodeIdentity keys — serving startup, live churn, and reconcile alike (ADR-0008/0009)
rationale: Three independent mutation paths would each need to handle node creation, deletion, and dedup, and any divergence between them produces ghost or duplicate nodes; a single SceneDiff keyed on stable NodeIdentity is the structural fix that makes all three scenarios share one correct implementation. Stable identity keys let the diff distinguish a node that persists from one that should spawn or despawn, and childless rig/pool parents cascade on despawn so no orphan containers linger. Routing startup, churn, and reconcile through the one engine means correctness is established once. This is an architectural call captured across two ADRs.
outcome: No ghost or duplicate nodes; childless rig/pool parents cascade on despawn; 30 pure-unit suites green; acceptance E2E ran twice green (scene-ready 363 ms, 2 towers / 15 lanes).

context: On reconnect/reconcile the client compares a desired set against live snapshots to decide despawns. The supervisor's list endpoint has a measured lag of ~40s, so a node briefly missing from a single snapshot may simply be late, not actually gone. Despawning on first absence would delete nodes that are merely lagging.
options: Despawn a node as soon as it is absent from a snapshot (risks deleting nodes that are only lagging behind the ~40s list-lag)
AbsenceDebounce — defer existence despawn until the node is absent on N=3 consecutive snapshots at a 20s reconcile cadence (a 60s window > the ~40s measured list-lag)
choice: AbsenceDebounce — defer existence despawn until the node is absent on N=3 consecutive snapshots at a 20s reconcile cadence (a 60s window > the ~40s measured list-lag)
rationale: Despawning on first absence is an irreversible delete triggered by what may be transient list-lag, so the debounce trades a small despawn delay for correctness against flapping. N=3 at a 20s cadence yields a 60s confirmation window, deliberately sized larger than the ~40s measured list-lag so a lagging node has time to reappear before it is removed. Despawn is made reconcile-driven only (not SSE-driven), concentrating the irreversible operation in the path that has the debounce guard. The window sizing is grounded in a measured lag rather than a guessed constant.
outcome: AbsenceDebounce defers despawn N=3 over a 60s window vs ~40s measured list-lag; reconcile consistent 0.4s after SSE re-establish; NFR legs (resume past N with straddle check) ran live green.

context: The reconciler's overflow/cold-open path re-snapshots through the diff engine when the seq cursor overflows the supervisor's retained window. At this build's store ceiling, a live window overflow could not be triggered, so the overflow-to-reconcile mechanism could not be exercised end-to-end on a live instance.
options: Leave the overflow-to-reconcile path untested because it is unriggerable live at this build's store ceiling
Unit-cover the overflow-to-reconcile mechanism (test_reconciler) since a live overflow is unriggerable
choice: Unit-cover the overflow-to-reconcile mechanism (test_reconciler) since a live overflow is unriggerable
rationale: An unverified path is a risk regardless of why it cannot be triggered live, so the mechanism is covered at the unit level rather than left to assumption when the live store ceiling makes an overflow unriggerable. Unit coverage exercises the overflow branch deterministically without depending on a store state the build cannot reach. The reason for the substitution (unriggerable at this build's ceiling) is recorded so the residual is explicit rather than a silent gap. The live NFR legs still exercise the reachable paths (burst at the store ceiling, resume past N).
outcome: Overflow-to-reconcile is unit-covered in test_reconciler; recorded as a build-verified finding; NFR burst ran live at the store ceiling and resume-past-N with straddle check ran green.
